title: The Parts, Devices, and Systems Hierarchy

lines.text: Section two. The parts, devices, and systems hierarchy.
Engineers, when they design anything non-trivial, almost always reach for a hierarchical decomposition.

They break the thing they want to build into modules, and those modules into smaller modules, and the smallest modules are built from standard, well-characterised components that can be ordered from a catalogue.
An aircraft is built from devices; the devices are built from parts; the parts come from suppliers' catalogues with detailed specification sheets.

A radio is built from a radio-frequency stage, an intermediate-frequency stage, and an audio amplifier; each stage is itself built from resistors, capacitors, and transistors; each component comes with a datasheet you can trust.
The discipline of choosing standard parts from a catalogue, combining them into devices, and combining devices into systems is what makes any kind of engineering tractable at scale.
Synthetic biology is the project of bringing this same discipline to biology.
The textbook calls this the Parts, Devices, and Systems approach, sometimes abbreviated as the P D S approach, and it underpins everything that follows.
Let me define the three layers cleanly.
First layer: parts, sometimes called bioparts.
A part is a stretch of DNA that encodes a single biological function.
A promoter is a part.
A ribosome binding site is a part.
A protein-coding sequence is a part.
A terminator is a part.
Each part is, currently, a specific sequence of DNA, and the field's job is to characterise that part — to know precisely how it behaves under different conditions and inside different host cells.
Second layer: devices.
A device is built by combining several parts in a defined way to produce a higher-level, human-meaningful function.

A simple example is a transcriptional unit: a promoter, plus a ribosome binding site, plus a protein-coding sequence, plus a terminator, assembled together produces a piece of DNA that expresses a specific protein when placed in a cell.

A more sophisticated device example is a logic gate, where the combination of several parts and proteins produces an input-output relationship that mimics a Boolean function — AND, OR, NOT, and so on — that you can chain into a circuit.
Third layer: systems.
A system is built from one or more devices and performs some task at the level of the whole cell — counting, oscillating, sensing and responding, controlling some downstream process.
You can see immediately that the hierarchy is recursive in the same way that electronic engineering's hierarchy is.
Three or four standard parts plus a defined arrangement give you a device.
Three or four devices plus a defined arrangement give you a system.
And — if synthetic biology continues to develop the way the authors hope — entire engineered cells will eventually be built up from standard systems with carefully managed interfaces.
What this approach gives you, conceptually, is the freedom to not build everything from scratch.

Concretely: to make a simple gene circuit that expresses a protein when an inducer is present, you do not need to design the promoter, the RBS, the coding sequence, and the terminator each from first principles.

You walk to the registry, find well-characterised parts that have already been measured under conditions similar to yours, combine them, and you have a working construct on the first try — at least in principle.
Three engineering principles undergird this whole approach, and they appear over and over.
Modularity: the ability to cleanly decompose a larger system into smaller, independent parts.
Characterisation: knowing rigorously how each part behaves — its inputs, its outputs, its dynamic range, its noise, its dependence on cellular context.
Standardisation: agreeing on shared interfaces so that parts from different sources can be combined.
Each of these is essentially aspirational in synthetic biology today.

Real bioparts are messier than electronic components — biology, as we said in chapter one, does not have the clean physical isolation of a printed circuit board, and parts interact with each other in ways that depend on their context inside the cell.

But the discipline of trying to enforce modularity, characterisation, and standardisation is, the authors argue, what will make synthetic biology mature into a real engineering discipline rather than remain a craft.

Once you accept the parts-devices-systems framing, the rest of the chapter becomes natural: how do we organise these parts, characterise them, store the data, model them, and design with them? Those are the next questions we tackle.
